justification: All rubric criteria met: MD5 identified as cryptographically broken and unsuitable for security tokens with specific attack vectors cited; HMAC-SHA256 recommended as replacement with explanation of why HMAC over raw hash; timing attack via string equality identified with crypto.timingSafeEqual recommended; timestamp design flaw thoroughly noted (validation is impossible without the timestamp); safe_to_deploy false. Additionally caught length-extension attack and input validation issues beyond the rubric requirements.

observations.text: MD5 correctly classified as Critical with specific references to collision/preimage attacks and NIST deprecation
Explicitly recommends HMAC-SHA256 and explains why keyed HMAC is needed over naive hash(message+key)
Timing attack identified with correct fix (crypto.timingSafeEqual with length check)
Timestamp design flaw identified as Critical — correctly notes validation is fundamentally broken
Length-extension attack via naive concatenation identified as a separate important issue
Suggests JWT as a standard self-contained token format
safe_to_deploy is false with clear rationale

trials.justification: All rubric criteria met: MD5 identified as cryptographically broken (Critical severity, references NIST/PCI-DSS/OWASP prohibitions); HMAC-SHA256 explicitly recommended with correct rationale (keyed MAC vs bare hash); timing attack via === identified as Critical with crypto.timingSafeEqual fix; timestamp design flaw noted (function returns only token, caller can't recover timestamp for validation); safe_to_deploy false.

observations.text: Correctly distinguishes hash vs HMAC as fundamentally different primitives, noting length-extension attack vulnerability in hash(msg+secret) pattern
Timing attack finding includes practical reproduction detail (10-50ns per character, ~10k requests for statistical analysis)
Identifies the unenforced 30-day expiration as a third Critical finding — goes beyond rubric expectations
Notes input validation issue with delimiter collision (partnerId containing ':') — subtle but real concern
Production readiness section addresses migration complexity of changing hash algorithm on existing tokens
Suggests JWT as a comprehensive fix that addresses all findings simultaneously

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: MD5 identified as cryptographically broken with collision/preimage attacks noted; HMAC-SHA256 explicitly recommended as replacement with correct code sample; timing attack via === identified as Critical with crypto.timingSafeEqual recommended; timestamp design flaw thoroughly covered (lost timestamp, missing expiry check); safe_to_deploy false. Also caught bonus issues: length-extension attack on hash(msg||key), input validation ambiguity, and suggested JWTs as alternative.

observations.text: MD5 finding is precise — mentions collision attacks, preimage attacks, and hashcat feasibility, not just 'weak'
Correctly identifies this as a hash-not-HMAC problem with explicit HMAC-SHA256 code fix and explanation of why HMAC is the correct primitive
Timing attack finding includes practical exploitation detail (1000 requests per byte position) and correct fix with Buffer.from + length check caveat
Timestamp issue thoroughly covered — identifies both the lost timestamp problem AND the missing expiry enforcement
Length-extension attack on concatenation pattern identified as separate important issue — goes beyond rubric requirements
Production readiness section notes migration risk for already-issued tokens — shows operational awareness
